variables.content:
For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

At each density, spin polarization, and finite system size the discrete DMC band values \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) (where \\(k=|\\mathbf{k}|\\)) were fitted over an extended wavevector interval to a quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4},
      $$
      with fitted coefficients \\(\\alpha_{0},\\alpha_{2},\\alpha_{4}\\). The quasiparticle effective mass was then obtained from the definition
      $$
      m^{*}=\\frac{k_{F}}{\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}},
      $$
      where \\(k_{F}\\) is the Fermi wavevector of the infinite 2D HEG at the given density and spin polarization and the derivative is evaluated at \\(k=k_{F}\\). It was observed that extracting the derivative from fits restricted to a very narrow interval immediately surrounding \\(k_{F}\\) yields ill-behaved (pathological) estimates of \\(m^{*}\\), whereas performing the quartic fit over a larger modest wavevector range around \\(k_{F}\\) produces stable, systematically varying estimates of \\(m^{*}\\). Averaging the band behaviour over such a modest \\(k\\)-range therefore provides a controlled practical procedure to suppress the finite-cell and Hartree–Fock-like near-\\(k_{F}\\) fluctuations that cause the pathological behaviour.

Consider the reported DMC calculations carried out with finite DMC time steps (time steps of 0.04, 0.2, and 0.4 a.u. at \\(r_{s}=1,5,10\\) for paramagnetic HEGs and 0.01, 0.2, and 0.4 a.u. at \\(r_{s}=1,5,10\\) for ferromagnetic HEGs) and using single-determinant Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions that, according to extrapolation of variational Monte Carlo energies to zero energy variance, retrieve more than 99% of the correlation energy. The proposition is: the residual biases arising from the fixed-node approximation and from finite DMC time-step errors (noting that halving the time step was verified to have a negligible effect on the computed energy band) are small compared with the overall uncertainties introduced by the finite-size extrapolation procedure; therefore these residual numerical biases do not materially shift the reported extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\).

For DMC-computed discrete single-quasiparticle bands \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) obtained in finite periodic simulation cells for a 2D HEG, define the pathological behaviour as the erratic variation of estimated quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}\\) when the derivative \\(d\\mathcal{E}/dk\\) is estimated by fitting only a very narrow \\(k\\)-range immediately surrounding the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: this near-\\(k_{F}\\) pathological behaviour of \\(m^{*}\\) estimates arises primarily from finite-cell fluctuations (for example, the discrete sampling of \\(\\mathbf{k}\\)-points and cell-induced finite-size correlation effects) together with Hartree–Fock-like non-smooth mean-field features that persist in finite cells, rather than from an intrinsic non-differentiability or singularity of the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle band at \\(k_{F}\\); this explains why expanding the \\(k\\)-range used in the fit reduces the pathology.

For discrete DMC single-particle band data E(k_i) in a periodic finite simulation cell, performing a quartic fit over a moderately wide wave-number interval k∈[k_F−Δk,k_F+Δk]—meaning Δk large enough to include multiple quantized k points on both sides of k_F rather than being restricted to an infinitesimal neighborhood of k_F—reduces sensitivity to localized anomalous fluctuations or outlier derivative estimates in the immediate vicinity of k_F and produces per-size effective-mass estimates that are more stable with respect to statistical noise and more amenable to controlled finite-size extrapolation.

The quasiparticle effective mass m^{*} inferred from the Galilean-invariance relations m^{*} = 1 + F_1^{s} for the paramagnetic case and m^{*} = 1 + F_1 for the ferromagnetic case, when evaluated using the extrapolated F_1^{s}/m^{*} and F_1/m^{*} values obtained from Fourier projection of DMC Landau interaction functions and N^{-1/4} finite-size extrapolation, was compared with effective masses obtained directly from fits to the single-particle energy band \mathcal{E}(k) extracted from independent DMC calculations; the two measures of m^{*} agree within statistical uncertainty in some cases (for example, the paramagnetic r_s = 5 case) but disagree by a statistically significant margin in roughly half of the cases. A concrete example is the ferromagnetic r_s = 10 case, for which the direct energy-band fit gives m^{*} = 0.67(4) while the value of m^{*} inferred from the F_1 relation and the extrapolated F_1/m^{*} is 0.493(7). The most likely origin of these disagreements is the large uncertainty associated with finite-size extrapolation of the Fourier-projected Fermi liquid parameters.

For the same HEG systems and trial wave functions defined above, applying the three-parameter extrapolation model
      $E_N = E_\infty + b_1\,\Delta T_{\mathrm{HF}}(N) + b_2/N$
      to DMC energies but fixing the finite-size coefficients $b_1$ and $b_2$ to the values obtained from VMC fits produces extrapolated DMC thermodynamic-limit energies that are spuriously low compared to extrapolations obtained by fitting $b_1$ and $b_2$ directly to the DMC data: using VMC $b_1,b_2$ yields DMC extrapolations DMC-SJ* $E_\infty^{\mathrm{DMC-SJ*}} = 0.00266(1)$ Ha/e and DMC-SJB* $E_\infty^{\mathrm{DMC-SJB*}} = 0.001380(8)$ Ha/e, whereas fitting $b_1,b_2$ to the DMC data gives DMC-SJ $E_\infty^{\mathrm{DMC-SJ}} = 0.00301(1)$ Ha/e and DMC-SJB $E_\infty^{\mathrm{DMC-SJB}} = 0.001621(7)$ Ha/e; therefore the hypothesis that VMC and DMC share identical finite-size dependences ($b_1,b_2$) is invalid for these systems and reusing VMC parameters biases DMC $E_\infty$ downward beyond the quoted uncertainties.

Assume the true thermodynamic-limit single-quasiparticle band of a 2D HEG is a smooth function \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) in a neighbourhood of the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: fitting the finite-cell discrete DMC band values to the quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4}
      $$
      over a modest interval \\([k_{F}-\\Delta k,\\,k_{F}+\\Delta k]\\) (with \\(\\Delta k\\) chosen so that the interval is not extremely small and contains sufficient discrete \\(k\\)-points to characterise curvature) yields an estimate of the derivative \\(\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}\\) that is an adequately accurate approximation to the true thermodynamic-limit derivative for the purpose of computing the quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}=k_{F}/(d\\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{F}}\\), provided the quartic form captures the local curvature of \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\).

The occupied single-particle energy band $\mathcal{E}(k)$ of the interacting two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) was computed using the diffusion quantum Monte Carlo (DMC) method by taking total-energy differences on adding or removing a single electron in a plane-wave orbital $\exp(i\mathbf{k}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{r})$; the resulting discrete DMC band values for the occupied states were described by a least-squares fit to the quartic form $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\alpha_{4}k^{4}$, where $\alpha_{0}$, $\alpha_{2}$, and $\alpha_{4}$ are fitted coefficients.  These DMC calculations were performed for both the paramagnetic (unpolarized) and the ferromagnetic (fully spin-polarized) 2D HEGs at density-parameter values $r_{s}=1$, $5$, and $10$ (atomic units, where lengths are given in Bohr radii).  The fitted quartic shows clear nonquadratic behavior: for the paramagnetic fluid $\alpha_{4}>0$ at $r_{s}=5$ and $r_{s}=10$, for the ferromagnetic fluid $\alpha_{4}<0$ at all three densities studied, and for the paramagnetic fluid at $r_{s}=1$ the fitted DMC band is very close to the free-electron dispersion $\mathcal{E}(k)=k^{2}/2$ (all energies in Hartree atomic units).
